opening-hero.title: Self-Improving Software
opening-hero.subtitle: How recursion and dogfooding collapse the complexity of building tools that build themselves

what-is-self-improving.title: The Pattern You've Already Seen
what-is-self-improving.body:
A compiler written in the language it compiles. A linter that follows its own rules. A deck generator that was designed using the decks it generates.

Self-improving software isn't magic — it's the deliberate application of two old ideas to the same problem at the same time:
bullets: Recursion — the system operates on itself
Dogfooding — the builder is also the user
Together: the feedback loop closes, and complexity collapses

relationship-branch.title: How do you relate to the tools you build?
relationship-branch.subtitle: Pick the path that fits your context — they all converge.

branches.label: I use my own tools daily
branches.desc: Dogfooding is already part of my workflow

branches.label: I build for other people
branches.desc: My users aren't me — feedback comes from outside

branches.label: I think in systems and abstractions
branches.desc: I want the theoretical lens first

dogfooder-path.title: You Already Have the Tightest Loop
dogfooder-path.body:
When you use your own tool every day, latency-to-feedback approaches zero. You feel the rough edges before you consciously notice them.

The strategy: make your tool a dependency of the workflow that produces the tool. Not just as a test — as a hard dependency that breaks your build if the tool regresses.
bullets: Use the tool in its own CI pipeline
Write your documentation with the tool
The tool must process itself to ship

user-builder-path.title: Close the Loop Artificially
user-builder-path.body:
If you're not your own user, you can still create a recursive structure. The trick: build the scaffolding that generates the inputs your tool consumes.

Now the scaffolding tool and the core tool improve each other. You've synthetic-dogfooded your way into a self-referential system.
bullets: Build a fixture generator — then improve it with the main tool
Use your own API to write your own docs
Your onboarding flow is a test of your own product

systems-thinker-path.title: Fixed Points and Strange Loops
systems-thinker-path.body:
In math, a fixed point is where f(x) = x — the function applied to itself produces itself. Self-improving systems aim for this: the tool's output, when fed back in, improves the tool.

Hofstadter called these strange loops. They feel like magic, but they're just well-placed feedback with delayed evaluation.
systems-thinker-path.code: // The ideal self-improving function function improve(tool) { const output = tool.run(tool.source()); return tool.applyPatch(output); // f(f) → better f }

recursive-loop.title: The Loop That Collapses Complexity
recursive-loop.body:
Here's why this works at scale: every improvement to the tool improves the tool's ability to improve itself. You get compounding returns without compounding code.

Traditional software growth: more features → more surface area → more maintenance. Recursive growth: more capability → better outputs → outputs improve inputs → tighter loop.
bullets: Complexity is paid once, at the abstraction layer
New capability comes from the tool using itself in new ways
Maintenance burden shrinks as the tool learns its own shape

this-deck-meta.title: This Talk Is the Example
this-deck-meta.body:
The deck you're watching was generated by deck-engine — a branching presentation tool. The CLI that generates decks was designed by giving Claude a deck about how the CLI works.

The generator improved every time it was used to explain the generator. No separate test suite. No synthetic fixtures. The tool was the test.
bullets: deck generate README.md → draft deck spec
Present the deck → notice gaps
deck refine deck.json "add a branch for X" → close the gap
The README improves. The prompt improves. The output improves.

hard-parts-branch.title: What's the hardest part for you?
hard-parts-branch.subtitle: Both paths lead to the same practical patterns.

branches.label: Getting the first loop started
branches.desc: Bootstrapping feels circular — what comes first?

branches.label: Knowing when to stop iterating
branches.desc: How do you avoid infinite regress or scope creep?

bootstrap-path.title: Bootstrapping: Cheat on the First Generation
bootstrap-path.body:
The first version doesn't need to be self-produced. It just needs to be good enough to produce something you can critique.

Write v0 by hand. Use it once, on itself, even if the result is bad. Now you have a real input and a real output — the loop has started. Every subsequent version is recursive.
bullets: Hand-write v0 — don't over-engineer it
Run v0 on its own source or docs immediately
Treat the bad output as the first bug report
v1 is the first self-produced artifact

termination-path.title: Termination: Ship the Fixed Point
termination-path.body:
Infinite loops are bugs. Recursive improvement needs a termination condition. The right one: does another pass through the tool produce a meaningfully different result?

When the tool's output on itself stabilizes, you've reached the fixed point. Ship it. Resume when the domain changes.
bullets: Diff the output of two consecutive passes
If delta < threshold: you've converged
Resume iteration only when inputs change (new feature, new audience)
Stability is a feature, not stagnation

practical-patterns.title: Three Patterns to Steal Today
practical-patterns.body: These work regardless of stack, language, or team size:
bullets: Pattern 1 — Use your tool in its own README generation. If it can't explain itself, it's not ready.
Pattern 2 — Pipe your tool's output back as its own input. Any breakage is a regression test you didn't have to write.
Pattern 3 — Write your tool's prompt or config using the tool. The meta-document becomes the canonical integration test.

getting-started.title: The Three-Step Starter
getting-started.body: You don't need a grand design. You need a first loop.
bullets: Step 1: Build something small that solves your own problem
Step 2: Use it to produce one artifact related to itself (docs, config, test data)
Step 3: Use that artifact to improve the tool — then repeat from step 2
getting-started.code: # This is the whole methodology while tool_can_improve(): artifact = tool.run(tool.self_description()) tool = tool.apply(artifact) ship(tool)

closing-hero.title: Build the tool. Use the tool. Improve the tool.
closing-hero.subtitle: Recursion isn't a technique — it's a relationship between a builder and their work. Dogfooding closes the loop. Together, they make complexity compound in your favor.
